meta.name: PHQ-A Adolescent Depression Screen
meta.description: The PHQ-A is an adolescent-adapted version of the PHQ-9 depression screening tool, using age-appropriate language to assess depressive symptoms in teenagers, plus two functional impairment items.
meta.author: Johnson JG, Harris ES, Spitzer RL, Williams JBW (2002)
meta.reference: Johnson JG, et al. The Patient Health Questionnaire for Adolescents. J Adolesc Health. 2002;30(3):196-204.
meta.instruction: Over the last 2 weeks, how often have you been bothered by any of the following problems?

questions.text: Feeling down, depressed, irritable, or hopeless
Little interest or pleasure in doing things
Trouble falling asleep, staying asleep, or sleeping too much
Poor appetite, weight loss, or overeating
Feeling tired, or having little energy
Feeling bad about yourself, feeling that you are a failure, or that you have let yourself or your family down
Trouble concentrating on things like school work, reading, or watching TV
Moving or speaking so slowly that other people noticed, or being so fidgety or restless that you move around more than usual
Thoughts that you would be better off dead, or of hurting yourself in some way
In the past year, have you felt depressed or sad most days, even if you felt okay sometimes?
Has being bothered by these problems made it difficult for you to do your work, take care of things at home, or get along with other people?

ranges.label: Normal
ranges.description: Your depression score is within the normal range. This indicates that your mood has been relatively stable recently, with no significant signs of persistent sadness or loss of interest.
suggestions: Continue maintaining a healthy lifestyle
Keep up with activities you enjoy
Talk to a trusted adult if you ever feel down
Maintain regular social activities and hobbies
Set aside time each day for activities you enjoy

ranges.label: Mild Depression
ranges.description: You may be experiencing some mild depressive symptoms. These mild symptoms may occasionally affect your energy and motivation, but they typically do not interfere significantly with daily functioning.
suggestions: Talk to a parent, teacher, or school counselor
Stay active and connected with friends
Maintain a regular sleep schedule
Try 30 minutes of aerobic exercise daily, such as walking or cycling
Maintain a regular sleep schedule and avoid staying up late

ranges.label: Moderate Depression
ranges.description: Your depressive symptoms are at a moderate level and may be affecting school and daily activities. At this level, feelings of low mood may cause persistent fatigue and reduced interest in activities, potentially affecting daily life and relationships.
suggestions: Talk to a trusted adult about how you feel
Consider seeing a school counselor or therapist
Stay physically active
Keep a regular routine
Consider consulting a mental health counselor for professional guidance

ranges.label: Moderately Severe Depression
ranges.description: Your depressive symptoms are quite significant. Please talk to someone you trust. Your score suggests notable emotional distress that may be significantly affecting your quality of life. Reaching out to a mental health professional is recommended.
suggestions: Please talk to a parent, teacher, or counselor soon
A mental health professional can help
If you have thoughts of hurting yourself, tell someone right away
Schedule an appointment with a mental health professional as soon as possible
You can call a mental health helpline for immediate support

ranges.label: Severe Depression
ranges.description: Your depressive symptoms are very severe. Please reach out to a trusted adult or professional right away. Your score suggests notable emotional distress that may be significantly affecting your quality of life. Reaching out to a mental health professional is recommended.
suggestions: Please tell a parent, teacher, or trusted adult immediately
If having thoughts of self-harm, call a crisis line
You are not alone - help is available
Schedule an appointment with a mental health professional as soon as possible
You can call a mental health helpline for immediate support
